event: The Jomon period begins, marked by hunter-gatherer communities producing some of the world's earliest pottery.
The Yayoi period begins, introducing wet-rice agriculture and bronze and iron metallurgy from the Asian mainland.
A ruler of the Wa state of Na receives a gold seal from the Chinese Han emperor, the earliest documented contact with China.
Queen Himiko of the Yamatai polity sends envoys to the Chinese Wei court, as recorded in Chinese chronicles.
The Kofun period begins, with powerful clans building massive keyhole-shaped burial mounds and the Yamato state consolidating power.
Buddhism is officially introduced to Japan from the Korean kingdom of Baekje, transforming religion and art.
Prince Shotoku promulgates the Seventeen-Article Constitution, establishing moral and political principles for governance.
The Taika Reforms centralize state power along Chinese imperial models, reorganizing land and administration.
The capital is established at Nara (Heijo-kyo), beginning the Nara period and a flourishing of Buddhist culture.
The Kojiki, Japan's oldest surviving chronicle of myth and history, is compiled.
Emperor Kanmu moves the capital to Heian-kyo (Kyoto), beginning the Heian period of aristocratic court culture.
Murasaki Shikibu writes The Tale of Genji, often considered the world's first novel.
Minamoto no Yoritomo defeats the Taira clan in the Genpei War, establishing warrior rule.
Yoritomo is named shogun, founding the Kamakura shogunate and Japan's first warrior government.
The first Mongol invasion is repelled, aided by storms later mythologized as the 'kamikaze' divine wind.
A second, larger Mongol invasion fails when a typhoon destroys Kublai Khan's fleet.
Ashikaga Takauji establishes the Muromachi shogunate, ushering in a long era of cultural patronage and instability.
The Onin War erupts, plunging Japan into the century-long Sengoku period of warring states.
Portuguese traders arrive, introducing firearms and beginning European contact and the spread of Christianity.
Oda Nobunaga overthrows the last Ashikaga shogun, accelerating the reunification of Japan.
Toyotomi Hideyoshi launches the first of two failed invasions of Korea.
Tokugawa Ieyasu wins the decisive Battle of Sekigahara, securing national dominance.
Tokugawa Ieyasu becomes shogun, founding the Edo shogunate and over two centuries of stability.
The shogunate completes its sakoku isolation policy, closing Japan to most foreign contact.
Commodore Matthew Perry's American 'Black Ships' arrive, pressuring Japan to end its isolation.
The Convention of Kanagawa opens Japanese ports to American trade, ending centuries of seclusion.
The Meiji Restoration topples the shogunate and restores imperial rule, launching rapid modernization.
The Meiji Constitution is promulgated, establishing a constitutional monarchy with a parliament (Diet).
Japan wins the First Sino-Japanese War, gaining Taiwan and emerging as a regional power.
Japan defeats Russia in the Russo-Japanese War, becoming the first Asian power to defeat a European empire in modern times.
Japan annexes Korea, beginning 35 years of colonial rule over the peninsula.
The Great Kanto Earthquake devastates Tokyo and Yokohama, killing over 100,000 people.
Japan invades Manchuria and establishes the puppet state of Manchukuo.
The Second Sino-Japanese War begins, including the atrocity of the Nanjing Massacre.
Japan attacks Pearl Harbor, bringing the United States into World War II.
Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki precede Japan's surrender, ending World War II.
A new pacifist constitution takes effect, renouncing war and establishing democratic government.
The Treaty of San Francisco restores Japanese sovereignty, ending the Allied occupation.
Japan is admitted to the United Nations, rejoining the international community.
Tokyo hosts the Summer Olympics and launches the Shinkansen bullet train, symbolizing postwar recovery.
The United States returns Okinawa to Japanese administration.
Emperor Hirohito dies, ending the Showa era and beginning the Heisei era under Emperor Akihito.
The collapse of the asset price bubble ushers in the 'Lost Decade' of economic stagnation.
The Great Hanshin earthquake strikes Kobe and the Aum Shinrikyo cult carries out a sarin attack on the Tokyo subway.
A massive earthquake and tsunami trigger the Fukushima nuclear disaster, the worst since Chernobyl.
Emperor Akihito abdicates and Naruhito ascends the throne, beginning the Reiwa era.
Tokyo hosts the pandemic-delayed Summer Olympics, held largely without spectators.
Former Prime Minister Shinzo Abe is assassinated during a campaign speech, shocking the nation.
